behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real OS file-watching behavior (recursive watch/event emission across nested directories). MOCK-MISS: this is not just parser logic; it is the watcher->daemon contract for nested path events. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: in-process fakes can emit idealized events and miss platform-specific watcher semantics. Rejected lower level: behavior text explicitly depends on real watched directory structure ('inside a subdirectory of the watched root').
behaviors.test_description: Start the real daemon against a real temp directory tree, create a nested subdirectory, write a file inside it, and assert exactly one transformed output appears for that nested file path within timeout.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is a local dedup rule over (path, time window), with no required OS/network/subprocess/LLM boundary. MOCK-MISS check: concrete failure can be exposed by directly invoking dedup state transitions in one object/function. Rejected higher levels: behavior text is limited to 'same file path within a configurable time window' and does not require real filesystem event semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate dedup component with a known window and fake clock, submit same path twice within window, assert first accepted/second rejected; advance clock beyond window and assert third accepted.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly targets OS duplicate event emission. REAL-INFRA: exact duplication patterns are produced by real watcher/OS behavior. MOCK-HIDE: mocked watcher often emits one clean event and can hide real duplicate-edge failures. Rejected Integration: text explicitly says 'operating system emits multiple filesystem events'.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with real watcher, perform one physical file write known to trigger multiple events on the platform (or repeatedly append/close pattern), and assert only one pipeline execution/output for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Failure is at OS watch-handle lifecycle boundary (directory deletion/recreation invalidates/rebinds watch state). REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: fake FS/watchers usually do not model inode replacement and watcher invalidation accurately. Rejected Integration: behavior text explicitly requires remove+recreate while process stays running.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on real directory, delete watched directory, recreate it at same path, then add a new file and assert it is processed without restarting daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on real filesystem metadata/timestamp resolution plus event ingestion timing. Cross-module contract: FS timestamp retrieval -> scheduler queue ordering must be preserved. REAL-INFRA: creation-time semantics vary by OS/filesystem and can break ordering in practice. Rejected Integration: behavior text ties order to real 'creation timestamps' at arrival time, not just in-memory sort logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files near-simultaneously on real filesystem with controlled creation times (or measurable offsets), run daemon, and assert processing/output order matches filesystem creation timestamps.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This is a real file I/O race between writer and watcher/processor. REAL-INFRA: partial writes, flush timing, and file-close visibility are OS/filesystem behaviors. MOCK-HIDE: synthetic events with prebuilt file content can hide premature-read bugs. Rejected Integration: behavior explicitly depends on concurrent real write completion timing.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running, open a file for incremental writes, trigger arrival while file is incomplete, verify daemon does not process intermediate content, then close/finish write and assert single processing occurs with full final content.
